title: K-Means vs Hierarchical vs DBSCAN: Which to Use?
concept: K-Means is fast but needs K and assumes round clusters, hierarchical clustering needs no K but is slower and builds a full tree, and DBSCAN needs no K and finds odd shapes while flagging noise — the right choice depends on your data and goal.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which algorithm groups points by density and marks isolated points as noise, with no K required?
exercises.explain: Right! Today you'll compare DBSCAN against K-Means and hierarchical clustering to decide when to use each.

cards.title: Three tools, one shed
cards.body: A carpenter doesn't use a hammer for every job — sometimes it's a saw, sometimes a drill. K-Means, hierarchical clustering, and DBSCAN are three different clustering tools, and knowing when to grab each one matters just as much as knowing how each one works.

cards.title: Three approaches, side by side
cards.body: K-Means assigns every point to the cluster of its nearest centroid, using K center points chosen in advance. Hierarchical clustering merges the closest groups step by step into a tree, letting you pick the number of clusters afterward. DBSCAN groups densely packed points and labels sparse points as noise, with no need to choose K at all.

cards.title: Cutting a pizza three different ways
cards.body: K-Means is like cutting a pizza into exactly K equal slices before you even see the toppings. Hierarchical clustering is like slowly cutting the pizza piece by piece and deciding afterward how many slices to keep. DBSCAN only cuts out the parts where the toppings are piled thick, leaving the bare crust untouched.

cards.title: Same store, three questions
cards.body: A retailer segmenting shoppers would use K-Means if they already know they want exactly 5 marketing segments and need results fast. They'd use hierarchical clustering if they want to explore the data's structure and decide the number of segments later. They'd use DBSCAN if they suspect a few shoppers are wild outliers that shouldn't be crammed into any segment.

cards.title: There's no single 'best' algorithm
cards.body: You might expect one clustering algorithm to always win. In reality, each one trades off differently: K-Means is the fastest but needs K chosen in advance and assumes roughly round, similarly sized clusters. Hierarchical clustering skips the K choice and reveals structure at every level, but is slower on large datasets. DBSCAN skips K too and handles oddly shaped clusters and noise, but can struggle when clusters have very different densities.

cards.title: Pick K-Means or Pick the Others

left.text:
You already know K
Need speed on big data
Clusters are round & similar-sized

right.text:
You don't know K
Want structure (Hierarchical)
or need noise-handling (DBSCAN)

exercises.prompt: Which clustering algorithm requires you to choose the number of clusters (K) before it runs?
exercises.explain: Correct — K-Means needs K picked in advance. Hierarchical and DBSCAN both skip that requirement.

exercises.prompt: True or false: DBSCAN can find clusters of unusual, non-round shapes and mark stray points as noise.
exercises.explain: True — DBSCAN traces out clusters of any shape based on density and explicitly labels outliers as noise.

exercises.sentence: Hierarchical clustering builds a full tree of merges, letting you choose the number of clusters ___ the algorithm finishes.
exercises.explain: Hierarchical clustering builds the whole dendrogram first — you decide how many clusters you want by cutting the tree afterward.

exercises.prompt: A data scientist has a huge dataset, already knows she wants exactly 4 customer segments, and needs results in seconds. Which algorithm best fits this situation?
exercises.explain: Right — when K is already known and speed matters most, K-Means is the natural choice.

exercises.prompt: You're analyzing sensor readings from a factory floor. You don't know how many distinct operating patterns exist, some readings are clearly faulty, noisy outliers that shouldn't be grouped with anything, and the patterns don't necessarily form neat round clusters. Which algorithm is the best fit, and why?

options.text: DBSCAN, because it needs no K, can trace oddly shaped clusters, and explicitly labels outlier readings as noise
K-Means, because it's the fastest algorithm regardless of shape or noise
Hierarchical clustering, because it's the only algorithm that handles noise
Any of the three, since they all handle noise and shape identically

exercises.explain: Exactly! DBSCAN was built for exactly this situation: unknown cluster count, irregular shapes, and noisy outliers to filter out. Next: seeing these algorithms show up in real domains like biology and social networks.
